what: Sleep paralysis — waking into a body that won't move, with a partial dream-state still active — has independently generated creatures across cultures: the Old Hag (Newfoundland), the Mora (Slavic), Kanashibari (Japan), Incubus/Succubus (medieval Europe), and the Night Mare (origin of 'nightmare' — a mare that rides you). All are the same physiological event given a body and a name.
insight: The mythological creature is often more accurate than the clinical description. 'Sleep paralysis with hypnagogic hallucinations' tells you the mechanism. 'Something is sitting on you in the dark' tells you what it is like. Mythology as phenomenology: the felt reality of an experience, formalized into culture. This is part of what makes mythology such a strong vessel — it encodes lived experience in a form that transmits across generations.

connections.relation: specific instance — a Lusatian Sorb death-messenger whose parallel house-spirit shares the same etymology as 'nightmare'
